title: Add hbctool._bitcodec C++ extension and route integer encode/decode through it
body:
### Motivation

- Reduce Python-level overhead for small integer/byte conversions by moving little-endian integer encode/decode paths to native code for speed and lower CPU cost.

### Description

- Added a new C++ extension `hbctool/_bitcodec.cpp` which implements `le_to_uint` and `uint_to_le` helpers for little-endian integer decoding/encoding up to 8 bytes.
- Wired `hbctool/util.py` to discover/load the new `_bitcodec` extension when `HBCTOOL_FASTUTIL=1` and to route `readuint` and `writeuint` through `_bitcodec` for sizes up to 8 bytes (falling back to the Python code when the extension is not available).
- Updated `setup.py` to build and ship both `hbctool._fastutil` and the new `hbctool._bitcodec` extensions.

### Testing

- Ran the full test suite with `python -m pytest -q`, which passed (`19 passed`).
- Built extensions with `python setup.py build_ext --inplace`, which succeeded and produced both `_fastutil` and `_bitcodec` shared objects (note: an existing compiler warning from `_fastutil.cpp` remains). 
- Exercised the fast runtime path by running `HBCTOOL_FASTUTIL=1 python -m pytest -q tests/test_util.py`, which revealed two failing tests due to a pre-existing exception-type mismatch in `_fastutil.to_uint16` error handling (the failure is unrelated to `_bitcodec` logic).

------
[Codex Task](https://chatgpt.com/codex/cloud/tasks/task_e_69d5f50380e0832d8e057dcb7cc75333)

title: Build index_shuffle C++ extension in setuptools wheel
body:
### Motivation
- Ensure the `index_shuffle` pybind C++ extension is built and packaged when making wheels so Python imports of `grain._src.python.experimental.index_shuffle.python.index_shuffle_module` succeed in non-Bazel / pip workflows.

### Description
- Add a setuptools `Extension` in `setup.py` to compile `index_shuffle_module.cc` and `index_shuffle.cc` into `grain._src.python.experimental.index_shuffle.python.index_shuffle_module` when building wheels.
- Add `pybind11>=2.12` to `pyproject.toml` build-system `requires` so isolated PEP 517 builds can discover pybind11 headers.
- Replace the `absl::StrCat` usage in the pybind module with `std::to_string` and include `<string>` to avoid pulling Abseil C++ into the setuptools-only build path.

### Testing
- Ran `python -m pip wheel . -w /tmp/grain-dist --no-deps` and the wheel build completed successfully.
- Inspected the produced wheel with a small Python script and confirmed `grain/_src/python/experimental/index_shuffle/python/index_shuffle_module*.so` is present inside the wheel, indicating the extension was packaged.

------
[Codex Task](https://chatgpt.com/codex/tasks/task_e_69d4e1e8eaec832493544ad994bbcd5d)

title: Berry solidification made possible with Python runtime
body:
## Description:

The Berry runtime was ported from `C` to `Python` which allows to move solidification stage into the toolchain. The goal is eventually to introduce new features like conditional compilation (smaller firmwares) and text localization.

The porting took less than a week using Kiro and Claude Opus 4.6. It generates a byte-to-byte compatible solidification source code. 

The solidification is not integrated in the toolchain yet.

As a side effect, `int64` support was ported to plain `C` code not using `lv_mapping` which makes it also faster.

## Checklist:
  - [x] The pull request is done against the latest development branch
  - [x] Only relevant files were touched
  - [x] Only one feature/fix was added per PR and the code change compiles without warnings
  - [ ] The code change is tested and works with Tasmota core ESP8266 V.2.7.8
  - [x] The code change is tested and works with Tasmota core ESP32 V.3.1.10 / V.3.3.7
  - [x] I accept the [CLA](https://github.com/arendst/Tasmota/blob/development/CONTRIBUTING.md#contributor-license-agreement-cla).

_NOTE: The code change must pass CI tests. **Your PR cannot be merged unless tests pass**_

title: feat(lang): add C and C++ grammar support
body:
## Summary

Add C and C++ grammar support via tree-sitter-cpp. One feature flag (lang-cpp, included in default), one shared handler module (cpp.rs) for both 'c' and 'cpp' language names, seven file extensions registered in the autodetection pipeline.

## Changes

- `Cargo.toml` -- add tree-sitter-cpp 0.23.4 to workspace dependencies
- `crates/code-analyze-core/Cargo.toml` -- add lang-cpp optional dep and feature; add to default list
- `crates/code-analyze-core/src/lang.rs` -- register .c, .h, .cpp, .cc, .cxx, .hxx, .hpp in EXTENSION_MAP and supported_languages()
- `crates/code-analyze-core/src/languages/mod.rs` -- register pub mod cpp; add 'c' | 'cpp' arms in get_language_info() and get_ts_language()
- `crates/code-analyze-core/src/languages/cpp.rs` -- new handler: ELEMENT_QUERY, CALL_QUERY, REFERENCE_QUERY, IMPORT_QUERY, find_method_for_receiver, extract_inheritance; 6 unit tests

Capture names aligned with all other language handlers (@func_name, @class_name, @call, @type_ref, @import_path).

## Test plan

- [x] cargo test passes (293 total, 6 new C++ tests: free function, class method, struct, include directive, template function, inheritance)
- [x] cargo clippy -- -D warnings clean
- [x] cargo fmt --check clean
- [x] cargo deny check advisories licenses clean
- [x] Capture names cross-checked against fortran.rs, python.rs, rust.rs, go.rs, java.rs

Closes #605

title: Move sensor metadata from C++ to Python schema (fixes compilation with ESPHome 2026.3)
body:
ESPHome 2026.3 removed undocumented `EntityBase` runtime setters such as `set_unit_of_measurement()` and `set_device_class()`. This component still used those setters in several custom sensor constructors, which caused the build failure.

This PR migrates those sensor metadata fields to the Python schema/codegen layer and removes the obsolete C++ setter calls.

Affected sensors:
- `CompressorFrequencySensor` (Hz, frequency)
- `InputPowerSensor` (W, power)
- `kWhSensor` (kWh, energy)
- `RuntimeHoursSensor` (h, duration)
- `OutsideAirTemperatureSensor` (°C, temperature)

## Notes

This follows the migration path expected by ESPHome 2026.3: entity metadata is now provided through schema/codegen and packed into `configure_entity_()` instead of being set from runtime C++ constructors.

⚠️ **This is not a cosmetic change.** The sensor metadata path changes entirely: from C++ runtime to Python codegen. Please verify carefully:

- [x] All five sensors appear correctly in Home Assistant with proper unit and device_class (not "unknown" or blank)
- [x] Historical data / long-term statistics are not disrupted (same entity IDs, same units; HA should see continuity)
- [x] YAML overrides still work (e.g. setting a custom `unit_of_measurement` on any of these sensors)
- [x] No regressions on compressor_frequency reporting during active heating/cooling cycles
- [x] kWh sensor still uses `state_class: total_increasing` (critical for energy dashboard)

It would also be good to test it with older ESPHome versions to confirm backward compatibility. I would expect it to remain compatible, but it’s still worth verifying explicitly.

title: Add Doodle cpp binding
body:
This pull request adds native support for rendering Doodle origami diagram files to PostScript directly from Python, and introduces a robust build and packaging system for cross-platform distribution. The main changes include integration of the Doodle C++ codebase as a submodule, new Python bindings via pybind11, high-level Python APIs for rendering, and automated build workflows for wheels and source distributions.

**Native Doodle integration and rendering support:**

* Added the Doodle C++ project as a submodule (`doodle`) and configured it in `.gitmodules`. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-3bfc46640be4fe9e435f96aef61db5a6bacff92ba0198546f8ca321542980946R1) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-fe7afb5c9c916e521401d3fcfb4277d5071798c3baf83baf11d6071742823584R1-R3)
* Implemented a pybind11 extension (`pydoodle/_bindings.cpp`) to expose the Doodle parse/render pipeline to Python, allowing `.doo` files to be rendered to PostScript from Python.
* Provided a high-level Python API (`pydoodle/render.py`) with `render` and `render_file` functions for converting `Diagram` objects or `.doo` files to PostScript.
* Updated the package interface (`pydoodle/__init__.py`) to expose the new rendering functions. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-e655c067101d793da8f44b83c5cf88998b967dcf7d03e9dd252b284c0b3a45c7R3) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-e655c067101d793da8f44b83c5cf88998b967dcf7d03e9dd252b284c0b3a45c7R86-R87)

**Build and packaging system:**

* Added `setup.py` to build the C++ extension using pybind11 and setuptools, including handling for generated parser/lexer files and cross-platform compiler settings.
* Introduced `pyproject.toml` for modern PEP 517/518 build configuration, specifying dependencies and cibuildwheel settings for multi-version Python wheels.
* Created a GitHub Actions workflow (`.github/workflows/build.yml`) to automate building wheels and source distributions for all major platforms and publishing to PyPI on tagged releases.

**Documentation improvements:**

* Extended `README.md` with detailed build, installation, and parser regeneration instructions, including platform-specific notes for developers.

title: Move Gaussian splatting autograd and pipeline logic from C++ to Python
body:
## Summary

- Port all C++ autograd functions (projection, rasterization, SH evaluation) to pure Python `torch.autograd.Function` implementations in a new `_gaussian_autograd.py` module, making the differentiable pipeline easier to extend and debug.
- Eliminate the C++ `GaussianSplat3d` class entirely: the Python `GaussianSplat3d` class now directly manages `torch.Tensor` attributes instead of delegating to a C++ `_impl` object.
- Rewrite `deduplicatePixels` as a pure Python function using PyTorch ops, removing its C++ binding and implementation.
- Refactor PLY I/O to return a flat tuple of tensors (no intermediate struct), and update the viewer to accept individual tensors directly instead of a C++ object.
- Remove ~7,600 lines of dead C++ code (`GaussianSplat3d.{h,cpp}`, `detail/autograd/*`, `DeduplicatePixelsTest.cpp`, `GaussianSplat3dCameraApiTest.cpp`).
- Simplify pybind bindings to only expose low-level dispatch functions; all autograd wiring now lives in Python.

## Test plan

- [x] All 135 Python unit tests pass (`cd tests && pytest unit/test_gaussian_splat_3d.py -v`)
- [x] All 21 C++ gtests pass (`ctest --test-dir build/.../src/tests`)
- [x] CI passes (codestyle, DCO, full test matrix)

title: Migrate MMHC from R/C++ to Python with Streamlit app and notebooks
body:
Replace the entire R package + Rcpp/C++ backend with a modern Python implementation using NumPy, SciPy, Numba, and NetworkX.

Key improvements over the original:
- No conditioning variable limit (was capped at 3)
- Cycle detection during hill-climbing (was missing)
- Reproducible via seeded RNG (was srand(time(NULL)))
- Configurable parameters via MMHCConfig dataclass
- 60 pytest tests at 92% coverage
- Streamlit app with Excel/CSV upload and interactive pyvis graphs
- Two Jupyter notebooks (quickstart + custom data)

title: Add C++ extension modules for CPU-intensive routines (pybind11)
body:
Implement compiled C++ replacements for the highest-impact performance bottlenecks in freqtrade, organized into 6 submodules:

S-tier (10-50x speedup potential):
- backtesting: Fast trade simulation engine (simulate_trades) replacing the inner backtest_loop with compiled OHLCV+signal array processing, ROI/stoploss/trailing-stop evaluation

A-tier (5-20x speedup potential):
- trade_math: Native double-precision profit calculations replacing FtPrecise string-based math (calculate_profit, calc_profit_ratio, calc_close_rate_for_roi, recalc_trade_from_orders, batch operations)
- data_processing: Fast NaN filtering (combined_nan_mask), feature shifting (shift_features), fillna+predict mask for FreqAI pipelines

B-tier (3-10x speedup potential):
- report_gen: Binary-search signal candle extraction replacing O(n^2) iterrows+concat loops, max drawdown calculation, pair metrics aggregation, expectancy calculation
- orderflow: Volume profile aggregation, imbalance detection, stacked imbalance finding, per-candle bid/ask/delta aggregation
- indicators: Heikin Ashi (eliminates row-by-row .at[] loop), rolling mean/std/sum/min/max, EMA

Architecture:
- freqtrade/cpp/src/*.cpp — C++ source with pybind11 bindings
- freqtrade/ft_cpp/*.py — Python wrappers with graceful fallback
- setup_cpp.py — Build script (python setup_cpp.py build_ext --inplace)
- All existing code paths fall back to pure Python if C++ not compiled

Integration points:
- qtpylib/indicators.py: heikinashi() uses C++ when available
- optimize_reports.py: signal candle extraction uses binary search
- freqai/data_kitchen.py: filter_features() and populate_features() use C++ NaN filtering and feature shifting

https://claude.ai/code/session_01235AT46ZzRkMthznH2BTMc

title: Port EMU7800 Atari 7800/2600 emulator from C# to Python
body:
Complete port of the EMU7800 C# emulator to Python, including:

- MOS 6502 CPU emulation (all 256 opcodes, addressing modes, BCD, interrupts)
- TIA graphics chip (2600 video rendering, collision detection, WSYNC)
- MARIA graphics chip (7800 video with DMA, 6 render modes, DLL/DL)
- PIA/RIOT 6532 (timer, I/O ports, 128B RAM, controller/console switch reading)
- AddressSpace with page-granularity memory mapping and snooper device
- InputState with double-buffered controller/console-switch state
- TIASound (2-channel) and PokeySound (4-channel) audio emulation
- 27+ cartridge mappers for both 2600 and 7800
- Machine hierarchy: MachineBase -> Machine2600/Machine7800 -> NTSC/PAL variants
- Platform layer: pygame window, audio output, keyboard/gamepad input handling
- Shell services: ROM loading with .a78 header parsing, machine factory
- CLI entry point with argparse for loading and running ROMs

https://claude.ai/code/session_01174A385inAc7io8bZKf2Ek

title: optimize C extension
[bugfix] Rewrite transfer metrics from C++ to Python side
body:
Previously, transfer metrics (ops_total, bytes_total) were recorded in the C++ layer (transfer.cu, transfer_ssd.cpp, gds_manager.cpp) via MetricsManager macros. Although C++ has its own Prometheus exporter (port 8081), the actual data transfer code runs in forked worker subprocesses, which do not initialize the C++ Prometheus metrics server. As a result, these transfer metrics were never collected at all.

Key changes:
- Rewrite transfer metrics collection entirely on the Python side with a post-completion approach, recording metrics in the main process after transfer operations complete
- Add `flexkv_py_transfer_bytes_total` counter to Python collector for tracking data transfer throughput
- Remove `flexkv_cpp_transfer_ops_total` and `flexkv_cpp_transfer_bytes_total` counters from C++ MetricsManager

Changes include:
- Extended `CompletedOp` in `flexkv/common/transfer.py` with `transfer_type`, `num_blocks`, and `num_bytes` fields
- Compute transfer bytes in `TransferEngine` (`transfer_engine.py`) when an op finishes
- Record all three counters (ops_total, blocks_total, bytes_total) via `record_transfer_completed()` in `KVTaskManager._update_tasks()` (`kvtask.py`), ensuring metrics are collected in the main process
- Added `flexkv_py_transfer_bytes_total` counter to `flexkv/metrics/collector.py`
- Removed old `_record_transfer_ops()` from `cache_engine.py`
- Removed C++ transfer metric recording from `transfer.cu`, `transfer_ssd.cpp`, and `gds_manager.cpp`
- Updated Grafana dashboard (`flexkv-demo.json`): added Transfer Throughput panel, replaced C++ Transfer Throughput with Cache Blocks Rate panel
- Updated monitoring documentation at `docs/monitoring/README_en.md` (English) and `docs/monitoring/README_zh.md` (Chinese)

title: Annex C Extensions
body:
https://github.com/walt-id/waltid-identity-enterprise/pull/382

- Multiple credentials for Annex C DC API
- Reader Auth (for multiple credentials) for Annex C DC API
- Various smaller fixes and changes for Verifier2

- This branch is based on https://github.com/walt-id/waltid-identity/pull/1590 and https://github.com/walt-id/waltid-identity/pull/1589 (primarily adding support for custom credential issuance (non-typesafe credentials) in the mdocs2 library, and updating the claim handling to no longer require explicit schemas upfront for received credentials)
  - Mdocs schema aware issuance
  - Massively rehaul CBOR handling, simplify serialization and deserialization (including removing multiple custom serializers), robust handling of non-primitives, better CBOR tagging support
  - Rethrow error to avoid sending random 404 responses with no data
  - Remove incorrect OpenAPI parameter for session info
  - Fix buildFarJar for hoplite

- Adds support for using the normal GeneralFlowConfig options for Annex C (e.g. for webhook notifications)
- Removed random wrappers in Annex C flow config, so that it can be used almost the same as the other flow configs (e.g. regarding policies)

---
Changes API of Annex C in Verifier2 slightly to support multiple credentials, new API is:
```json
{
  "flow_type": "dc_api-annex-c",
  "requestedElements": {
    "org.iso.18013.5.1.mDL": {
      "org.iso.18013.5.1": [
        "family_name",
        "..."
      ]
    }
  },
  "origin": "https://digital-credentials.walt.id"
}
```
Note how there is no `"docType"` attribute, and instead the credentials are named in `requestedElements` (which also feels more natural, as this is also the format that they are being represented with in other parts).

* **Bug Fixes**
  * Improved hash-mismatch reporting and claim-path resolution for complex DCQL/SD-JWT cases; tightened null-safety in test extensions.

* **Chores**
  * Standardized dependencies via version catalog; upgraded test tooling; bumped OpenAPI libs; removed experimental API opt-ins; simplified serialization handling.

title: Fix source fallback for case-variant C/C++ extensions
body:
## Summary
- fix derive_source_file to treat file extensions case-insensitively when inferring fallback source filenames
- preserve original source name when unit names end with .C, .Cpp, .CpP, etc., instead of rewriting to .cpp
